text:
Been through this through of sorrow more than once and found two things helpful: 
1) peer group of founders 
2) a goal outside of work that WAS under my control 

I regularly met with 3 other founders for dinners and we created a WhatsApp group. Being able to ask for advice was good, but more than anything it was important to have someone to talk to who “gets it”. 

I set ambitious fitness goals. When going through due diligence for an acquisition that took months and could fall apart any moment I decided to learn how to handstand (“if this falls apart, at least I will know how to handstand”). Another time, Seed round close extended by ~6 months due to legal complications (setting up a tech startup in Germany was a really dumb idea) so I got into advanced calisthenics movements. 

What was great about these fitness goals is that they gave me a sense of control. At work I could do everything right and still go down in flames, but putting in the effort at the gym gave guaranteed results.

What was great about these fitness goals is that they gave me a sense of control. At work I could do everything right and still go down in flames, but putting in the effort at the gym gave guaranteed results.

text:
It’s high time that there is a mechanism to keep parasitic publications accountable and highlight high quality ones, I think this could be built on top of X’s social graph: 

1. Auth with X and rate publications you’re familiar with, add evidence of low quality reporting/lies.
2. Use something like https://t.co/sQmQEnKu7B scores to weigh votes and counteract vote manipulation, it can also help surface sentiment towards publications in given communities (eg tech, crypto). 

I think this should be open source and non profit. Someone interested to work on this?

2. Use something like https://t.co/sQmQEnKu7B scores to weigh votes and counteract vote manipulation, it can also help surface sentiment towards publications in given communities (eg tech, crypto). 

I think this should be open source and non profit. Someone interested to work on this?

text:
Living organisms respond to abundance by expanding, scarcity by shrinking. 

But what happens when the information system breaks? That's exactly what happens in Type 2 Diabetes. https://t.co/rO5FFPaRds

raw.full_text:
Living organisms respond to abundance by expanding, scarcity by shrinking. 

But what happens when the information system breaks? That's exactly what happens in Type 2 Diabetes. https://t.co/rO5FFPaRds

text:
It's a recipe for a vicious cycle (positive feedback loop):

False scarcity signals mislead the system
Organism/society responds defensively
Defense mechanisms distort signals further
Cycle accelerates

If not broken in time, this loop ends in system collapse. https://t.co/yAM3qzbf4Q
